summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW — PARENTING AND PROPERTY —Interlocutory application — With whom the children live and time arrangements — Allegations of alcohol misuse — Allegations of coercive control — Spouse maintenance —Injunctions — Partial property settlement — Disclosure —Where proceedings are listed for final hearing in three months — Where the parties are separated under the same roof — Where there is significant imbalance in the parties’ financial positions — Where the wife seeks partial property settlement to fund her legal representation at trial —— Interim parenting, property and procedural orders made.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CHRISTIE J:

The proceedings concern interlocutory parenting and property issues in the spectre of a final hearing which is listed for 5 days commencing 29 June 2026:
(a) Interim parenting;

(b) Exclusive occupation;

(c) Spouse maintenance;

(d) Injunctions; and

(e) Sale of property and interim distribution.

It is necessary to begin by recording that the applicant (“wife”), respondent (“husband”) and Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) all agree that it is in the interests of the children that the parents assume separate residences. This accords with the Child Impact Report writer’s recommendation. What follows is that it is necessary to consider where each of the adults will live (and the attendant financial arrangements) as well as the configuration of time and orders for decision making which will best meet the needs of the children on an interim basis.

Interim parenting

The children are X (age 14), Y (age 13) and Z (age 9).
The arrangements for the children’s care prior to the parties’ separation were largely that the wife was primarily responsible for their day-to-day care while their husband worked. The husband was otherwise an involved parent. The parties do not agree about the balance of care arrangements in the latter part of the relationship and post-separation.
The parties separated in about May 2024. They have remained living under the same roof since separation. This arrangement has ensured a continuity in the time the children see each parent, but it has also come at a cost to all members of this family.
The wife seeks an order that the children live with her and spend time with the husband. The husband’s primary position is that the children should live with him and spend time with the wife. The husband also seeks that the Court make some injunctions. The husband’s alternate application (following submissions) was that the children should live in a week-about arrangement.
The husband contends that since November 2025, he has been primarily responsible for the children’s care. The wife agrees that since November 2025 the children have been transported to school, appointments and extra-curricular activities primarily by the husband as she has been without the means to do so. The parties are at odds about whether this change to arrangements amounts to a shift whereby the children have been in the husband’s primary care since November 2025.
It is clear that after 2020 the husband is likely to have been more involved in the children’s care as a consequence of the COVID-19 pandemic and increased work from home.
Prior to this, the parties’ evidence acknowledges that while the husband was involved, the wife was primarily responsible for parenting. The husband’s work as legal professional involved trials, preparation and travel (including staying in the Central Business District and interstate during trials). The husband’s work travel has lessened.
The husband’s application is in large part reliant upon the evidence he asks the Court to accept about risk – evidence which he contends makes the arrangements which he proposes more suitable for the children.
The husband says that the wife’s alcohol consumption and mental health are risk factors. The evidence does not explain the precise nature of the risk. The assertion of risk sits uncomfortably with the husband’s final relief (equal time) and his alternate interim relief (equal time).
It is necessary to canvas both of these issues in detail.

Alcohol use/misuse

The husband raised alcohol consumption in interview for the Child Impact Report. The report records at [44]:
Mr [Delgato] has expressed ongoing concern regarding Ms [Delgato’s] drinking, noting that she drinks daily and has previously consumed alcohol excessively.
The wife told the Child Impact Report writer that she drank alcohol three to four times per week. In her affidavit, the wife denies a problem with alcohol consumption.
The husband’s concerns are set out in his affidavit filed 20 June 2025 where he details his observations of the wife’s drinking during the last few years of their relationship, including one third to half a glass of vodka before bed following her consumption of other drinks in the afternoon/evening.
He is concerned, based on financial records, that the wife continues to drink to excess but keeps any alcohol in her own room in the house. He says that she has asked him to take the children to school on a number of occasions due to migraine.
Exhibit 11 demonstrates transactions by the wife at liquor stores totalling $9,241.31 in the period of 12 September 2024 to 15 April 2025. The figure appears high and I cannot discount the possibility that it is indicative of problematic level of alcohol consumption by the wife during that period. It is more difficult to interpret the wife’s expenditure in the period of 15 April 2025 to 20 November 2025 (Exhibit 12). During that period the wife’s transactions at liquor stores totalled $1,872.64. The husband submits that while her purchases at liquor stores had decreased from 15 April 2025, her cash withdrawals had increased such that I would infer that the wife was using the cash to purchase alcohol. I cannot discount the possibility that some alcohol was purchased with cash. If all the cash withdrawn was used to purchase alcohol in this period, then that would continue to demonstrate a level of consumption which may be problematic. I was not taken to evidence relating to the wife’s cash withdrawals prior to April 2025.
The husband does not give evidence of any incident or event - whether prior to separation or after separation - in which he says he observed the wife inebriated or observed the effects of alcohol on the wife’s parenting.

Mental health

The wife’s evidence is that she suffers from anxiety and takes medication. She is not presently seeing a psychologist. She attributes that to her finances. She attached a letter from a General Practitioner, Dr F dated 7 November 2025 which says:
Mrs [Delgato] has been suffering from the Chronic Insomnia, Chronic Anxiety Disoder [sic] and Recently Depression. She has attended our clinic regularly for the treatment and advice. She is on antidepressant medication and the dose has been increased recently. She has had the Mental Health Care Plan in place and she is going to see the psychologist / hypnotherapist at the end of this month.
The husband instructed his lawyers to propose to the wife that he meet the gap between the fee charged by her psychologist and the amount covered by a Medicare mental health plan in December 2025. There is no evidence before me that the wife instructed her lawyers to respond.
The wife has given lengthy evidence relating to her concern that she is required to account to the husband in respect of her expenditure including, as an example, his request for reimbursements of a $12 Uber ride. The wife appears to experience this as invasive. I cannot at this stage of the proceedings conclude that the husband’s requests are a form of financial control. That will, as I understand the wife’s case be the contention at trial. The husband for his part says there are cogent reasons for his financial actions and requests of the wife. At this interlocutory hearing, I interpret the financial issues as impacting upon the parties’ capacity to communicate and cooperate which is to the disadvantage of their children.
The husband gives evidence about an incident which occurred in November 2025 which resulted in a criminal charge against the wife. The wife says in her affidavit filed 9 December 2025 that Z came home holding a shrink-wrapped package which contained two steaks and said to her: “Dad says this is for us, I get one steak and Y and X get half each of the other.” The wife was upset that the husband had bought two steaks for the three children and expected her to cook them for the children. She accepts that she threw the steak but sets out the following interchange:
Husband: “You’ll get nothing until you sign the papers.”

[steak thrown by the wife]

Husband: “You are a violent cunt.”

[standing over wife – holding up finger and laughing]

Husband: “You’re not getting one dollar until you sign the documents.”
In November 2025, the husband reported this incident in person to the police in Suburb O. A Provisional Apprehended Violence Order (“ADVO”) was made on the same day and the police charged the wife with common assault. On the husband’s account the steaks made contact with his head. The husband’s affidavit filed 3 December 2025 says the wife screamed at him: “[Y]ou can shove the fucking steak up your ass” and “I cannot wait for you to die.” I am unable to determine on an interim basis which account is accurate. The wife entered a ‘not guilty’ plea to the assault charge at the Local Court hearing in December 2025.
This is not a case in which the husband says the wife has engaged in a course of violent conduct or coercion and control which has made him fearful. In fact he told the police he did not have fears. The situation in the jointly occupied home is unpleasant and untenable. I have some confidence that the situation will be ameliorated by the adults adopting separate residences.
The safety of the children is at the forefront of any decision about care arrangements since I am obliged to make orders which promote their safety.
The husband’s second affidavit filed 3 December 2025 detailed what he termed an escalation in the behaviour of the wife. There are a number of incidents set out: damage to a cake, water on his bed – which are denied by the wife. I cannot make a finding. Even if the wife was responsible, the incidents are petty and ill-considered but the husband does not say that the children were exposed to this conduct nor is it possible on the available evidence to conclude that these events are indicative of mental health concerns.
I am not satisfied that the evidence establishes that the children are or have been unsafe in the care of either parent. The central issue identified by the evidence is about their exposure to parental conflict.

The children’s views

The children have a close relationship with each parent. There is a Child Impact Report by a single expert in evidence before me. That report records the views expressed to the expert as at 11 March 2025.
It is unsurprising that the children, who have a good relationship with each parent, have expressed a desire for a shared arrangement. They have only known a household with both parents.
I was told from the bar table that the views of one of the children may have changed (based on a discussion with the ICL). It is important to understand that this Court can only adjudicate disputes on the basis of admissible evidence. A conversation between an ICL and a child is not a form of admissible evidence. Children’s views may change from time to time and these children will have the opportunity to speak to the single expert before the final hearing. The single expert will record those views and they will come into evidence.
Any decision I make about interim parenting arrangements may be revisited in less than four months.
At the commencement of the interim hearing, counsel of the husband indicated that he was instructed that his client proposed to move into the home of the maternal grandparents. This was not previously foreshadowed. There was no specific evidence about it. The matter was stood down to permit all the parties to consider whether it raised issues of procedural fairness. After the adjournment, the husband’s position was altered. Counsel for the husband indicated he no longer sought to occupy the home of the maternal grandparents with the children.
This is a complicated dynamic. The wife is presently estranged from her parents. The children have a good relationship with the maternal grandparents who live locally. The husband presently facilitates time between the children and the maternal grandparents.
The husband sought to rely on an affidavit by the maternal grandfather which was filed other than in accordance with the Court’s directions. I permitted him to do so as in my view the content was relevant to this determination.
The maternal grandfather’s affidavit details his availability to take the children to school, collect the children from school, care for the children before or after school and on weekends, and facilitate attendance at extra-curricular activities. Ideally such involvement would be firmly in the best interests of these children. Because of the estrangement between the maternal grandparents and the wife, the situation is more complex.
The wife submitted, and I accept, that the maternal grandfather’s evidence (and that of the husband) is to the effect that the husband would have the assistance of the maternal family, his adult children and nannies to facilitate his full-time care of the children notwithstanding the availability of the wife who has been a homemaker and parent throughout the children’s lives.
The ICL sought orders which would see the children live with the wife (in the former matrimonial home) and spend regular frequent time with the husband. This approach, it was submitted, would acknowledge the children’s long-term experience of having their mother as the person primarily responsible for their day to day care. The husband’s proposal, it was submitted, would constitute a substantial change to their arrangements.
I accept that it is important to consider what arrangements will best promote the children’s stability in the period between these orders and the final orders. The wife is remaining in the home. The husband will obtain rental premises in the area – having regard to the children’s school.
I am persuaded that the ICL’s proposal has too many changes of care each week. I am not confident that this will not introduce the possibility for complications. The husband says that his alternate proposal of a week-about arrangement will address this issue. I agree it would address that issue, but it would represent a marked departure from the care the children have experienced. I accept the husband has become more involved, but the wife is available to care for the children with no work commitments, while the husband could be expected to be focused on his work and business given the downturn he has detailed and the commitments he has to provide for the support of his school-aged children. It is not inappropriate for him to have the assistance of others (especially family), but the wife is available and, in the short term (absent a finding that the children would be unsafe), the children should live with the wife and spend time with the husband. To minimise changeover that time should be from Thursday after school to Monday before school each alternate week and for the second half of each school holiday period.
I canvassed with the parties their areas of agreement in respect of special occasions and my orders in that regard mirror the parties’ concessions.

Exclusive occupation

Ultimately the parties agreed that the wife should have exclusive occupation of the Suburb L property (being the property in which the parties and the children presently reside). The husband sought an order that he be required to vacate on or before 30 April 2026. That is too long. The single expert report said almost a year ago:
Transition to Separate Residences. Continued cohabitation post-separation has contributed to tension, blurred boundaries, and the risk of increased emotional stress for the children. As such, it is recommended that the parents establish two separate, stable households to support the children’s need for clear structure, emotional safety, and reduce exposure to conflict during this critical adjustment period.
The husband has volunteered to vacate but not until the end of April. He offered no specific reason as to why the children ought experience this position for another month and a half. I will make an order that he vacate within 28 days. Should he find alternate accommodation earlier, that is clearly preferable. I will make an order that his time with the children will commence when he has secured rental premises (not being the home of the maternal grandparents).

Injunctions – Wife’s partner

The wife is in a relationship with Dr G. They do not live together. The husband seeks the following injunction:
10. That, pursuant to Section 68B of the Act:

10.1. Pending the Husband vacating the [Suburb L] Property in accordance with Order 16 herein, the Wife be restrained by injunction from inviting Dr [G] to attend or come within 10 meters of the entry point to the [Suburb L] Property; and

10.2. Following the Husband vacating the [Suburb L] Property in accordance with Order 16 herein, the wife be restrained by injunction inviting Dr [G] to attend or come within 10 meters of the entry point to the [Suburb L] Property when the children are residing with the Wife; and

10.3. Consuming alcohol to excess and is to remain at all times under the blood alcohol level of 0.05% while the Children are in her care, and for a period of 24 hours prior to the Children coming into her care.
The wife resists the making of those injunctions.
The following events inform my conclusion about whether the evidence supports the making of an injunction.
On 12 December 2024 the husband was not at home in the evening. The wife invited Dr G to the home to have dinner and watch a movie. The wife sent the husband a text to let him know. The husband responded indicating that Dr G should be gone before the husband returned. The wife, Dr G and the children were outside when the husband returned. There was an interaction between Dr G and the husband.
On 6 February 2025 the husband and children were out for dinner. The wife had dinner with Dr G at the parties’ home. The husband and children returned and there was an interaction between the husband and Dr G. It is unclear why the husband’s affidavit filed 20 June 2025 refers to Dr G as the wife’s new de facto partner (at [15]) since it is plain that they do not live together.
On 20 April 2025 and 21 April 2025, the husband says Dr G was present at the Suburb L property which made him feel uncomfortable.
The husband’s evidence suggests that he does not want to encounter Dr G. The husband’s unease with Dr G’s presence at the former matrimonial home has led the husband to directly challenge Dr G about his presence. I am not in a position to make a finding about what occurred.
The wife has chosen to entertain Dr G at the parties’ home even though she knows it will cause tension and potentially expose the children to conflict. It is perplexing. The husband has chosen to address Dr G instead of ignoring him which is equally perplexing when viewed from the perspective of the children’s interests.
I am satisfied that the adults have acted inappropriately. I am not satisfied that there is a safety issue. I decline the application for an injunction.

Carbohydrate-Deficient Transferrin (“CDT”) testing and attendance by the wife on a psychologist

The husband seeks the following orders (Exhibit 1):
12. That, within 48 hours, the Mother is to undertake the following at her own cost (with the Mother to produce to the Father the results of such tests within 24 hours of receipt of same) chain of custody carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (“CDT”) testing at a [Q Medical service] or endorsed collection centre.

13. That the Mother is to undertake random CDT testing at her own costs and in the manner as required by Order 12 herein within 48 hours of being requested by the Father to do so (with the Mother to produce to the Father the results of such tests within 24 hours of receipt of same), such testing to only occur no more than once per calendar month.
In L v T [1999] FamCA 1699; (1999) FLC 92-875 the Full Court (Kay, Coleman & Brown JJ) considered an appeal against an order which required the wife to attend upon a psychiatrist. The Court said at [60]:
Had the ongoing psychiatric treatment been imposed as a condition of contact, then, in our view, on the evidence available to her Honour such an order could well have been upheld. However, the form of the order leaves it free standing, and in our view it does not comfortably fit within any of the heads of power that we have identified. In those circumstances we ordered that Order 13 be set aside.
In this case it is not proposed by the husband that the orders for CDT testing is a prerequisite to the children’s time with the wife as proposed by him.
The husband’s original application had sought that the wife attend upon a psychologist. The same order was not sought in Exhibit 1.
Self-evidently the wife proposes that she attend on a psychologist and it may be inferred that she and the children are likely to benefit from the treatment, but I decline to make an order (if one was in fact sought) in the circumstances of this case.
Similarly, while no submissions were directed to the issue of attendance at parenting courses, it is likely that both parents will derive benefit should they attend. I will not make an order but leave it to them to elect whether they attend.
While the husband requested that the wife voluntarily undertake CDT testing, he continued, following refusal, to seek an equal time arrangement. The evidence does not demonstrate any incident or event in which it is suggested there has been a risk to the safety of the children. I could not make a stand-alone order and I am not persuaded the evidence supports an order tied to the mother’s time.

Spouse maintenance

The wife seeks an order that the husband pay her $2,500 per week. She accepts that if she remains in the former matrimonial home, it will not be necessary and the sum of $1,500 would be appropriate. The average weekly expenses set out at Part N of her Financial Statement filed 11 November 2025 total $980.
The husband conceded that the wife satisfies the threshold in section 72 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) namely she is unable to support herself.
The husband says he does not have the financial capacity to meet her reasonable needs.
On or around 5 September 2024 the husband commenced paying the wife $1,000 for spouse maintenance plus $500 for the family’s groceries weekly. The husband ceased paying maintenance on or around 8 November 2025. The husband has continued to pay the wife $485 per week in accordance with a child support assessment.
The wife has borrowed money from a friend.
One of the most significant issues is whether I could, on the evidence, find that there had been an explicable change in the husband’s financial circumstances supported by evidence.
Rule 6.06(8) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) provides:
(8) A respondent to an application for maintenance only must bring to the court on the first court date the following documents:

(a) a copy of the respondent’s taxation return for the most recent financial year;

(b) a copy of the respondent’s taxation assessment for the most recent financial year;

(c) copies of the respondent’s bank records for the 12 months immediately before the date when the application was filed;

(d) the respondent’s most recent pay slip;

(e) if the respondent has an Australian Business Number—a copy of the last 4 business activity statements lodged;

(f) any document in the respondent’s possession, custody or control that may assist the court in determining the income, needs and financial resources of the respondent.
The husband has not completed his 2025 tax return. The husband has complied with Rule 6.06(8)(c) but some considerable time has passed since the wife’s application was filed.
The parties sought that I make case management orders and directions at the conclusion of the interim hearing. The wife sought disclosure orders. The husband did not object.
In Exhibit 15 the wife sought the following orders:
3 That within 7 days of the making of these orders, the husband provide by way of disclosure the following:

...

3.11 Bank statements (or transaction listings if statements not available) for the following accounts:

3.11.1. CBA Bank Account ending #...50 from 4 November 2025 to date.

3.11.2. CBA Bank Account ending #...82 from 4 November 2025 to date.

3.11.3. CBA Bank Account ending #...86 from 11 November 2025 to date.

3.11.4. V Bank Account #..82 from 1 July 2025 to date.

3.11.5. CBA Bank Account #...12 for the period from 11 November 2025 to date.

3.11.6. AMEX Credit Card #...07 for the period from 1 November 2025 to date.

3.11.7. CBA Credit Card #...10 from 11 November 2025 to date.
I made those orders. It is plain that, as at the date of this hearing, the husband has not provided the wife with the primary documents which are directly relevant to his present income/expenditure.
The most recent Financial Statement filed by the husband on 3 December 2025 indicated gross weekly income of $8,719 from his work as a legal professional, $1,100 from Suburb M rent and no income from his other business activities.
It is also significant that the husband has recorded his income in his Financial Statement after expenses – which does not allow for an analysis of the deductions, as they are presumably not recorded as expenses.
The notes to Part O of the husband’s Financial Statement indicate how he reached this estimate for weekly income and reads as follows:
1. Estimate gross income of $583,362 as follows:

a. January to March 2025 BAS: $97,295 (total sales exclusive of GST).

b. April to June 2025 BAS: $250,211 (total sales exclusive of GST).

In my Financial Statement filed 2 May 2025, I had estimated by BAS for the April to June 2025 period to be $94,794 (estimated total sales exclusive of GST). However, In June I had a large [matter] and believed I would issue an invoice in late June or July (which would be paid in the period July to September 2025). In or about mid-June [...] the client wanted an invoice. I issued said invoice on 19 June for $170,833.24, still expecting this to be paid in the period July to September 2025. However, it was paid on 26 June 2025. Therefore, payment of this invoice came in during the April to June 2025 quarter, rather than the following quarter.

c. July to September 2025 BAS: $135,856 (total sales exclusive of GST).

d. September to December 2025 BAS estimate: $100,000 (estimated total sales exclusive of GST).

2. After deducting business expenses totalling approximately $130,000 taxable income will be $453,362 (being $8,718.50 per week)
The husband’s most recent taxation return was for the year ending 30 June 2024. In that year the husband’s gross income was $1,069,411. There is no explanation for the significant diminution. The husband merely says he does not have much work.
The wife tendered the husband’s bank account statements for the Commonwealth Bank of Australia ‘[Bank]’ account in his name ending #...82 for the period of 1 September 2024 to 8 November 2025 (“the CBA account”). What is most significant about those statements is that the husband was receiving fees into this account until at least 15 November 2024 - at which time, the deposit on that day of $133,297 brought the credit balance to $382,886.30. Appropriately, the CBA account was drawn upon to pay substantial tax payments, but it did not appear to receive fee note income after November 2024 (only transfers or rent). It is not apparent where his fee income (if any) is being deposited.
I accept the husband has made payments to other members of his family (Exhibit 9). Bank transfers to Mr B (adult child of the husband) are regular and frequent. It is not plain if they continued after 2 November 2025 as there are no primary documents. In ordinary circumstances, payments to an adult child for gardening and other tasks might be unremarkable but here the husband has a duty to support the wife and those funds could be a source. Similarly, money has been transferred to Ms C as loans in August and September 2025 totalling $6,000. These transfers are unexplained by the husband and significant in light of the wife’s impecuniosity.
The husband’s credit card statements in evidence (Exhibit 8) are limited to the period 20 November 2024 to 18 March 2025. They do not demonstrate excessive spending by the husband on the credit card. There are no statements in evidence after this date.
The evidence upon which the husband relies (particularly that which it does not address) does not convince me that the husband lacks all capacity to pay spouse maintenance. If the husband’s shortfall is in fact as he indicates, then his expenditure of about $2,000 per week on the children (being Part N expense and child support) is unsustainable.
The husband has completed Part N in his own Financial Statement filed 3 December 2025. He says his personal expenses average $797 per week.
In circumstances where I have found that the husband’s disclosure is partial and has not addressed adequately his significant income reduction, the best measure of the amount which he should pay to the wife would appear to be an amount equivalent to that which he allocates to himself. Doing the best I can with the available evidence I find capacity and I will order that he pay the periodic amount of $800 by way of spouse maintenance. I accept that this may not address the wife’s reasonable needs but it should permit her to have some certainty about receipt of funds pending final hearing.

Interim property settlement

The wife seeks $100,000 as partial property settlement. The wife has already received $50,000. The husband’s final relief would not be jeopardised by the proposed payment to the wife – which is an acknowledgment by him of her entitlements by way of contribution and current and future financial circumstances.
I accept the argument that a further payment to the wife would appear to be appropriate having regard to the need to ensure that the parties are able to litigate from positions of approximate equality. Unlike the husband, the wife is not legally trained and has not practised law in this jurisdiction – she is reliant upon legal advice. The husband is, of course, entitled to be represented notwithstanding his own qualifications. He filed a Costs Notice which was before the Court which demonstrated that as at 9 March 2026, he has paid $126,124.56 by way of legal fees from his income (Exhibit 4); whereas, the wife has paid $39,685 as at 24 February 2026 (sourced from the $50,000 partial property settlement payment to her).
The difficulty in this case is identification of a specific liquid asset from which such a payment could be made.
The parties are the joint owners of three pieces of real property:
(f) H Street, Suburb L NSW (“[Suburb L]”);

(g) J Street, Suburb M NSW (“[Suburb M]”); and

(h) K Street, Suburb N NSW (“[Suburb N]”).

All three properties are encumbered. Suburb N (a commercial property) is on the market for sale. Suburb M is tenanted month to month, and the wife and children are to occupy Suburb L following the making of these orders.
The husband also has an interest in R Pty Ltd which will be the subject of a single expert valuation which is underway.
The wife seeks an order that the husband pay her $100,000. I cannot see a fund or liquid asset from which that could occur. If I were persuaded that there were likely to be such funds or liquid assets, I accept I could make that order even if there was some residual uncertainty. However, the evidence in this case does not permit that approach.
The Suburb N property is listed for sale. If the property is sold and settlement occurs, the wife should receive the funds from that source.
The wife seeks an order for sale of the Suburb M property. The wife has asked the husband to provide her with evidence that he can afford to retain the Suburb M property. The husband has not provided that evidence. The weekly mortgage repayments on Suburb M are $2,162 according to the husband’s Financial Statement filed 3 December 2025. In addition, the husband says the outgoings on Suburb M are $305 per week. The rent from Suburb M is $1,100 per week. Accordingly, the shortfall in respect of Suburb M is $1,367 per week.
Interestingly, if the husband were to earn income at the level he previously reported then such a shortfall would not pose any difficulty. If the husband’s income moving forward has declined to the extent he submits, then retaining the property may pose a challenge.
The husband seeks to retain the Suburb M property at a final hearing. Ironically, if the Court makes the finding about the husband’s income earning capacity at final hearing for which the wife contends, then he ought be easily able to retain the property.
Another factor which is persuasive is that even if I were to make an order for sale of Suburb M, it is most unlikely that the sale will have completed until the time of hearing.
While I accept the importance of a level-playing field, I am not persuaded that I should order sale of Suburb M at this stage.
I therefore propose to approach the issue in the following way.
The husband will be obliged to pay a dollar to the wife’s lawyer for every dollar he pays to his own lawyers between now and the final day of the hearing.
In addition, if the Suburb N property sells between now and the making of final orders, then the wife is to receive funds from that source.
The husband sought an order for the sale of the Suburb L property. I accept both parties seek sale at the final hearing. If I made an interim order, there is a possibility that the sale could occur before I deliver judgment in the final matter. The husband says that in that event, I ought make an order for the wife to receive cash from that sale which would then enable her to secure rental premises. The sale of the Suburb L property is not so urgent that I need to order its interim sale and I decline the application. Everyone will be aware of the final adjustment of property when final orders are made and they can provide for the orderly sale of Suburb L property.
I have no evidence about furniture and personalty and heard no submissions. I will make no order.

Further case management

The matter has been listed for final hearing and most of the filing directions were the subject of agreement between the parties.
The parties did not agree on payment for the single expert valuer. I indicated that in the absence of agreement as to value or approach, the Court may be left with no option but orders for sale. I will order that the husband pay in the first instance (consistent with my findings about his income and earning capacity expressed above).
The parties were also at issue about some disclosure issues. I indicated that the husband should provide documents in his possession and control related to the “R Group” as relevant to the issue of valuation. He should otherwise be obliged to provide any documents sought by the single expert. Any documents not in the possession or control of the husband may be sought on subpoena. Each party ought be free to make submission relating to costs arising out of the discovery process.
